title: Modern America (1991-Present)
desc: From the unipolar moment to a fractured era: tech revolution, 9/11, financial crisis, polarization, and pandemic.вЃ вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЃ

lessons.title: The Unipolar Moment and the 1990s
lessons.content:
The 1990s

Peace, prosperity, and quiet warning signs.

The decade

With the Soviet collapse (1991), the U.S. became the sole superpower. Charles Krauthammer (1990) called it "the unipolar moment." Francis Fukuyama (1989, 1992) argued liberal democracy might be "the end of history" вЂ” humanity's final form of government.

Major events:
вЂў Gulf War (1990-91) вЂ” coalition of 35 nations expelled Iraq from Kuwait in six weeks. Limited objectives, broad legitimacy.
вЂў NAFTA ratified (1993) вЂ” North American Free Trade Agreement.
вЂў Internet privatized and commercialized (1993-95) вЂ” Mosaic browser (1993), Netscape (1994), Amazon (1994), Yahoo (1995), Google (1998).
вЂў Welfare reform (1996, bipartisan) вЂ” work requirements, time limits.
вЂў Federal budget surplus (1998-2001) вЂ” first since 1969.
вЂў Bosnia (1995) and Kosovo (1999) вЂ” humanitarian interventions.
вЂў Y2K bug (Jan 1, 2000) вЂ” feared, mitigated.

Economy

Real GDP grew 38% during the 1990s. Unemployment fell to 3.9% (2000) вЂ” lowest since 1969. Median household income rose. The dot-com bubble inflated; the NASDAQ crashed in 2000-2002, losing 78% of its peak value. Many internet companies failed; survivors (Amazon, eBay) emerged dominant.

Warning signs ignored: Globalization concentrated gains in coastal metros; many manufacturing communities began long declines. Inequality widened. Financial deregulation (Gramm-Leach-Bliley, 1999) repealed Glass-Steagall (1933). Al-Qaeda attacks on U.S. embassies in Africa (1998) and the USS Cole (2000) foreshadowed 9/11.

"The end of history... the universalization of Western liberal democracy as the final form of human government."
вЂ” Francis Fukuyama, 1989 (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46) вЂ” a thesis subsequent decades have complicated.

quiz.q: What did Fukuyama mean by "the end of history"?
o: That liberal democracy and market economies might be the final, stable form of human political-economic organization
That the Cold War would never end
quiz.exp: Fukuyama drew on Hegel and KojГЁve: "history" in the sense of fundamental ideological conflict over how to organize society might be over, with liberal democracy as the universal answer. The thesis has been contested by the rise of authoritarian China, Islamism, and democratic backsliding вЂ” but also partially confirmed by the global spread of democratic norms since 1989.

lessons.title: September 11 and the War on Terror
lessons.content:
9/11 and the long war

A single day reshaped two decades.

The attack

On September 11, 2001, 19 al-Qaeda hijackers crashed planes into the World Trade Center, the Pentagon, and (Flight 93) a Pennsylvania field. 2,977 people died вЂ” the deadliest foreign attack on U.S. soil. Osama bin Laden took responsibility.

The response:
вЂў Authorization for Use of Military Force (Sept 14, 2001) вЂ” broad, vague.
вЂў Afghanistan invasion (October 2001) вЂ” Taliban government overthrown.
вЂў USA PATRIOT Act (Oct 26, 2001) вЂ” expanded surveillance powers.
вЂў Department of Homeland Security (2002).
вЂў Guantanamo Bay detention camp (Jan 2002).
вЂў Iraq War (March 2003) вЂ” based on claims of WMDs that proved false. Saddam Hussein toppled in three weeks; insurgency followed; ~4,500 American deaths and 100,000-600,000 Iraqi deaths in subsequent years.
вЂў Abu Ghraib torture revealed (2004); enhanced interrogation debate.
вЂў Bin Laden killed by SEAL Team 6 in Pakistan (May 2, 2011).
вЂў Afghanistan withdrawal (August 2021) вЂ” the Taliban returned to power.

The bill

Brown University's "Costs of War" project estimates the post-9/11 wars cost the U.S. $8 trillion and 7,000+ American military deaths; an estimated 300,000-900,000 deaths total across Iraq, Afghanistan, Pakistan, Syria, Yemen. The civil-liberties debate over surveillance, due process, and torture remains unresolved.

Hayek's warning, restated: Hayek argued (Constitution of Liberty, 1960) that emergencies tempt governments to expand powers that prove difficult to roll back. Twenty years after 9/11, much of the surveillance architecture remains.

"They have attacked America because we are freedom's home and defender."
вЂ” George W. Bush, September 11, 2001 evening address (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46)

quiz.q: What was the most consequential lasting effect of 9/11 on American institutions?
o: Major expansion of federal surveillance, executive war powers, and homeland security infrastructure
quiz.exp: 9/11 drove durable changes: the Department of Homeland Security, TSA, expanded NSA surveillance (revealed by Edward Snowden in 2013), broader executive war-making authority, and the long Afghanistan/Iraq engagements. Many of these structures remain in place decades later.

lessons.title: The 2008 Financial Crisis and the Great Recession
lessons.content:
The 2008 crisis

How a housing bubble nearly broke the world economy.

What happened

From the late 1990s, U.S. housing prices rose far above historical trends. Banks made mortgages вЂ” including "subprime" loans to high-risk borrowers вЂ” packaged them into mortgage-backed securities, and sold them globally. Credit-rating agencies misrated them. When housing prices peaked (2006) and started falling, the entire structure unraveled.

Timeline:
вЂў March 2008 вЂ” Bear Stearns rescued (sold to JPMorgan with Fed backstop).
вЂў Sept 7, 2008 вЂ” Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac taken into conservatorship.
вЂў Sept 15, 2008 вЂ” Lehman Brothers bankruptcy ($613B liabilities) вЂ” the moment panic spread.
вЂў Sept 16, 2008 вЂ” AIG rescued ($85B initial).
вЂў Oct 3, 2008 вЂ” TARP signed, $700B.
вЂў 2008-2009 вЂ” global recession. U.S. unemployment rose from 4.6% (2007) to 10% (Oct 2009).
вЂў Dodd-Frank Act (2010) вЂ” major financial regulation.
вЂў Federal Reserve cut rates to ~0%, launched QE (quantitative easing).

The debate

Causes are contested:
вЂў Too-loose monetary policy 2002-2005 (John Taylor; Austrian school).
вЂў Government distortion of housing markets via Fannie/Freddie, Community Reinvestment Act, mortgage interest deduction.
вЂў Wall Street recklessness and incentive failures (Michael Lewis, The Big Short).
вЂў Failure of credit rating agencies and securitization.
вЂў Global savings glut from China and oil exporters (Bernanke).
Most scholars cite a combination.

Aftermath: The recovery was slow. Trust in financial elites collapsed. Occupy Wall Street (2011) and the Tea Party (2009) emerged from opposite directions but with overlapping diagnoses: bailouts socialized losses while gains had been privatized. Populism on both left and right grew through the 2010s.

"In economics, things take longer to happen than you think they will, and then they happen faster than you thought they could."
вЂ” Rudiger Dornbusch (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46)

quiz.q: Why did the September 15, 2008 Lehman Brothers bankruptcy mark a turning point?
o: It was the first bank failure ever
Allowing Lehman to fail signaled there was no implicit government guarantee, triggering a cascading credit-market panic
It was completely unrelated to the crisis
quiz.exp: After Bear Stearns was rescued (March 2008), markets assumed similar firms would also be saved. Lehman's bankruptcy shattered that assumption, freezing short-term lending markets globally. Subsequent rescues (AIG, TARP) were efforts to contain the panic that Lehman's failure unleashed.

lessons.title: The Obama Years and Demographic Change
lessons.content:
The Obama era

First Black president; partisan polarization deepened.

Domestic policy

Barack Obama (2009-2017) inherited the financial crisis, two wars, and a $1.4T deficit. Major policies: American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (Feb 2009, $787B stimulus); Affordable Care Act (March 2010) вЂ” expanded health insurance to ~20 million; Dodd-Frank (2010); auto industry bailout (GM, Chrysler); recovery from 10% unemployment (2009) to 4.7% (Jan 2017); same-sex marriage legalized nationwide (Obergefell v. Hodges, June 2015).

Foreign policy: Iraq drawdown 2011 (returned in 2014 vs. ISIS); Afghanistan surge then drawdown; bin Laden killed (May 2011); Libya intervention (2011, no follow-through); Iran nuclear deal (JCPOA, 2015); Cuba opening (2014); Paris Agreement on climate (2015). Drone warfare expanded significantly.

Polarization

Despite Obama's 2008 promise to transcend partisan divisions, polarization deepened. The Tea Party (2009) opposed bailouts and the ACA. The Republican Party shifted right. Congressional cooperation collapsed. Filibusters became routine. Government shutdowns (2011, 2013) and debt-ceiling crises recurred. Pew Research documented a sharp rise in negative partisanship вЂ” Americans defining their views by what the other party opposed.

Demographic shifts: The 2010 Census showed continuing diversification вЂ” Hispanic population reached 50.5 million (16.3%), Asian population grew 43% in a decade. By 2014, public school enrollment became majority non-white. Religious affiliation declined: Christians fell from 78% (2007) to 65% (2019, Pew); the religiously unaffiliated rose from 16% to 26%.

"There's not a liberal America and a conservative America вЂ” there's the United States of America."
вЂ” Barack Obama, 2004 DNC keynote (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46) вЂ” an aspiration unfulfilled.

quiz.q: Which Supreme Court decision legalized same-sex marriage nationwide?
quiz.exp: In Obergefell v. Hodges (June 26, 2015), the Court ruled 5-4 that the 14th Amendment requires states to license and recognize same-sex marriages. Justice Kennedy wrote the majority opinion.

lessons.title: Populism, Polarization, and the Trump Era
lessons.content:
The 2016 inflection

A populist realignment in both parties.

The election

Donald Trump won the 2016 Republican primary against 16 rivals, then defeated Hillary Clinton 304-227 in the Electoral College while losing the popular vote by ~2.9 million. Bernie Sanders nearly won the Democratic primary on a democratic-socialist platform. Both insurgencies signaled discontent with post-Cold-War elite consensus on trade, immigration, and intervention.

Trump administration policies:
вЂў Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (Dec 2017) вЂ” major reduction in corporate and individual rates.
вЂў Three Supreme Court appointments (Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, Barrett) вЂ” solidified a 6-3 conservative majority.
вЂў Trade tariffs on China and steel/aluminum (2018+).
вЂў Immigration enforcement tightened; family separation at border (2018) drew bipartisan criticism.
вЂў Withdrew from Paris Agreement and Iran deal.
вЂў Abraham Accords (2020) вЂ” UAE, Bahrain, Morocco, Sudan recognized Israel.
вЂў U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA, 2020) replaced NAFTA.
вЂў First impeachment (Dec 2019, Senate acquitted Feb 2020) вЂ” Ukraine.
вЂў Second impeachment (Jan 2021, Senate acquitted Feb 2021) вЂ” Jan 6.

January 6, 2021

After Trump lost the November 2020 election (306-232 to Joe Biden, 7M popular vote margin), he and allies challenged results in many states. Over 60 court cases dismissed. On January 6, 2021, supporters stormed the Capitol during the certification of electoral votes. Five people died in connection with the events; ~140 police officers were injured. Congress completed certification that night. Over 1,500 people were charged.

The deeper currents: Many analysts (Patrick Deneen, Yuval Levin, Anne Applebaum, Francis Fukuyama, Ezra Klein) argue that institutional trust collapsed across decades вЂ” schools, media, churches, government, science. Polarization fed by social media algorithms, geographic sorting, and economic dislocation produced an electorate where compromise looked like betrayal.

"In the long run, we're all dead."
вЂ” John Maynard Keynes (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46) вЂ” but in the medium run, institutional decay has compounding costs.

quiz.q: What does the simultaneous strength of Bernie Sanders (left) and Donald Trump (right) in 2016 suggest about the American electorate?
o: Significant cross-party rejection of post-Cold-War elite consensus on trade, immigration, and foreign intervention
quiz.exp: Both candidates ran against the bipartisan consensus on free trade, mass immigration, and military intervention that had governed both parties for two decades. Their combined appeal вЂ” though their solutions differed sharply вЂ” signaled a realignment that has continued.

lessons.title: COVID-19 and Its Aftershocks
lessons.content:
The pandemic

A century-scale public health event with long economic consequences.

Pandemic timeline

SARS-CoV-2 emerged in Wuhan, China (late 2019). WHO declared a pandemic March 11, 2020. The U.S. declared a national emergency March 13, 2020. Lockdowns followed. Schools closed for many months in many states.

Pandemic data (U.S.):
вЂў ~1.2 million U.S. deaths (CDC, through 2024).
вЂў Unemployment spiked to 14.7% (April 2020) вЂ” highest since the Great Depression.
вЂў ~$5 trillion fiscal response (CARES Act, ARPA, etc.) вЂ” bipartisan and unprecedented in scale.
вЂў Federal Reserve balance sheet expanded from $4T to $9T.
вЂў Operation Warp Speed delivered approved vaccines in under one year (Pfizer/BioNTech and Moderna mRNA, Dec 2020) вЂ” historically rapid.
вЂў Schooling losses: NAEP scores in 2022 showed largest single drops on record in math and reading.
вЂў Inflation rose to 9.1% by mid-2022 (40-year high) вЂ” driven by supply shocks plus monetary and fiscal expansion. Federal Reserve raised rates aggressively (2022-23) to ~5.5%.

Social and political effects

The murder of George Floyd (May 25, 2020) triggered the largest protest movement in U.S. history вЂ” estimated 15-26 million participants in 2020 alone. Debates over policing, race, and inequality intensified. Remote work became normalized for ~30% of workers. Loneliness and youth mental health crises worsened. Trust in public health institutions split sharply along partisan lines.

The trade-off framing: Pandemic policy required choosing among imperfect options under uncertainty: lockdown severity, school closures, vaccine mandates, masking. Sweden, Florida, California, and New York followed different approaches with different outcomes. Honest retrospectives (Francis Collins, Anthony Fauci, Jay Bhattacharya, Marty Makary) acknowledge that some early decisions, especially prolonged school closures in Democratic-led states, produced costs that exceeded benefits вЂ” consistent with cost-benefit analyses by Hanke (2022) and others.

"Knowledge is in the end based on acknowledgement."
вЂ” Ludwig Wittgenstein (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46) вЂ” public health credibility depends on epistemic humility.

quiz.q: What contributed most directly to the 2022 inflation peak (9.1%)?
o: A combination of pandemic supply shocks, ~$5T fiscal expansion, and a Federal Reserve balance sheet that more than doubled
quiz.exp: The 2022 inflation was overdetermined: supply chains disrupted by COVID, energy shocks (made worse by Russia's invasion of Ukraine), unprecedented fiscal spending, and rapid Fed balance-sheet expansion. Mainstream and monetarist economists agree on the combination, while differing on weights.

lessons.title: Big Tech, Social Media, and the Information Environment
lessons.sub: How the internet changed politics and culture
lessons.content:
The platform era

Five companies reshaped public discourse.

The big platforms

By 2024, five U.S. companies вЂ” Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet (Google), Amazon, Meta (Facebook) вЂ” had a combined market capitalization above $10 trillion. Adding Nvidia and Tesla, the "Magnificent Seven" represented ~30% of the S&P 500.

Social media timeline:
вЂў Facebook (2004) вЂ” 3+ billion users by 2023.
вЂў YouTube (2005) вЂ” bought by Google 2006.
вЂў Twitter/X (2006) вЂ” bought by Musk 2022.
вЂў Instagram (2010) вЂ” bought by Facebook 2012.
вЂў Snapchat (2011), TikTok (international 2017).
вЂў Smartphones became ubiquitous after the iPhone (2007); 90%+ of U.S. adults had one by 2020.
вЂў Algorithmic ranking and infinite-scroll feeds came to dominate ~2012 onward.

The debate

Critics (Jonathan Haidt, Tristan Harris, Cal Newport) argue the algorithmic attention economy has driven youth depression and anxiety, polarized politics, eroded reading and attention spans, and degraded civic discourse. Haidt's The Anxious Generation (2024) ties the post-2012 mental health collapse to smartphone-based social media. Defenders argue that social media empowered marginalized voices, enabled organizing, broke information monopolies, and that correlation does not prove causation.

Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act (1996) shields platforms from liability for user-generated content. Both parties have proposed reforms. The First Amendment limits direct government regulation of speech. The structural question вЂ” who governs the digital public square вЂ” remains unresolved.

"Whoever controls the media, controls the mind."
вЂ” Jim Morrison, attributed (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46) вЂ” a frame that overstates determinism but captures the stakes of platform power.

quiz.q: What does Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act (1996) do?
o: It generally shields online platforms from civil liability for content posted by their users
It requires platforms to verify all user identities
quiz.exp: Section 230 distinguishes platforms from publishers: a website is generally not legally responsible for what its users post. Both parties debate reforming it, but it remains foundational to how the modern internet operates.

lessons.title: Inequality, Mobility, and the American Dream
lessons.content:
Inequality in modern America

Real trends, contested causes.

The data

From 1979 to 2019, real income grew across all groups вЂ” but unequally. The top 1% saw income roughly triple in real terms; the bottom 50% saw smaller gains. Wealth inequality rose more sharply than income inequality. The share of national income going to the top 10% rose from ~33% (1979) to ~46% (2019, World Inequality Database).

Mobility: Raj Chetty's research (Opportunity Insights, Harvard) finds that absolute mobility вЂ” the chance a child outearns their parents вЂ” fell from 90% for those born in 1940 to 50% for those born in 1980. Geographic mobility varies enormously: some counties (e.g., much of Utah, parts of the upper Midwest) deliver strong mobility; others (much of the rural South) deliver very little.

Causes (contested)

Mainstream explanations:
вЂў Skill-biased technological change вЂ” premium for college and STEM rose.
вЂў Globalization вЂ” manufacturing labor exposed to international competition (Autor, Dorn, Hanson "China Shock" research).
вЂў Decline of unions (private-sector union density: 35% in 1953 to 6% in 2023).
вЂў Tax policy вЂ” top marginal rates fell from 70% (1980) to 37% (2023).
вЂў Family structure changes вЂ” single-parent households rose; correlation with intergenerational poverty (Sara McLanahan, Charles Murray, Robert Putnam from different angles).
вЂў Housing scarcity in high-productivity cities вЂ” restrictive zoning (Glaeser, Hsieh-Moretti).

The optimistic view: Despite stagnation in median wages by some measures, broader measures of consumption show continued real gains: smartphones, healthcare technology, entertainment, food variety. Steven Pinker (Enlightenment Now, 2018) and Hans Rosling document continued global progress in life expectancy, literacy, and absolute poverty (which fell from 36% globally in 1990 to under 9% in 2019, World Bank).

"Most people stand on the shoulders of giants and don't know it."
вЂ” Deirdre McCloskey, paraphrased (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46) вЂ” modern Americans live richer than 99% of history's kings.

quiz.q: What does Raj Chetty's research show about absolute economic mobility in the United States?
o: It has steadily improved for every cohort
The probability that a child earns more than their parents fell from ~90% (1940 cohort) to ~50% (1980 cohort)
Mobility is uniform across the country
quiz.exp: Chetty's Opportunity Insights team uses tax data on tens of millions of families to track intergenerational outcomes. The decline in absolute upward mobility is one of the most cited findings; geographic variation in mobility is another central finding.

lessons.title: America in a Multipolar World (2017-2025)
lessons.sub: China, Russia, AI, and a contested future
lessons.content:
The new contest

The unipolar moment is over.

The geopolitical map

By the mid-2020s, three major contests defined U.S. foreign policy: China (economic and strategic competition), Russia (open conventional war in Europe), and Iran and proxy networks (Hamas, Hezbollah, Houthis). Both major U.S. parties have shifted toward greater concern about China, breaking from the 1990s-2000s engagement consensus.

Key developments:
вЂў Russia invaded Ukraine February 24, 2022 вЂ” largest land war in Europe since 1945. U.S. and NATO provided billions in aid; conflict ongoing through 2024-25.
вЂў Hamas attack on Israel October 7, 2023 вЂ” 1,200 killed, 250+ hostages taken. Israel-Hamas war followed. Regional escalation involved Iran, Hezbollah, the Houthis.
вЂў U.S.-China decoupling вЂ” CHIPS Act (2022) for domestic semiconductors; export controls on advanced AI chips (Oct 2022, expanded 2023).
вЂў Taiwan strait tensions remain a central concern; Taiwan produces ~90% of advanced semiconductors.
вЂў AUKUS pact (2021) вЂ” U.S., U.K., Australia security partnership.

The AI revolution

OpenAI's ChatGPT (Nov 2022) reached 100 million users in two months вЂ” the fastest consumer adoption in history. By 2024, large language models were transforming software development, customer service, education, and search. The leading developers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta, xAI) are all U.S.-based; China is investing heavily. Debate centers on alignment, safety, labor displacement, and intellectual property.

Energy and climate: The U.S. became the world's largest oil and gas producer (mid-2010s onward) thanks to fracking. Renewable share of U.S. electricity generation reached ~22% (2023). Emissions per capita fell while economy grew. Climate debate continues over speed, costs, and trade-offs.

"What is the use of decrying the past, when the present and the future stretch out before us, full of possibilities and full of perils?"
вЂ” Theodore Roosevelt, 1910 (paraphrased) (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46)

quiz.q: What development most directly ended the post-Cold-War consensus on engaging China economically?
o: A bipartisan reassessment driven by trade-deficit concerns, technology theft, the China Shock to U.S. manufacturing, and strategic competition over Taiwan and the South China Sea
quiz.exp: The shift had multiple drivers and crossed party lines: Trump-era tariffs (2018), Biden-era export controls (2022-23), and the CHIPS Act (2022). Underlying it were Autor-Dorn-Hanson's China Shock research and rising concern about Beijing's military buildup and Hong Kong/Taiwan.

lessons.title: Modern America in Retrospect (1991-Present): Synthesis
lessons.content:
Modern America in retrospect

A republic continuing to navigate its founding tensions.

The arc

From 1991 to 2025, the United States went from sole superpower at the end of history to a polarized democracy in a multipolar world facing China's rise, deep institutional distrust, and rapid technological change. It also grew richer in absolute terms; absorbed 50+ million immigrants; legalized same-sex marriage; survived a pandemic; led the AI revolution; and remained the world's largest economy.

Ten lessons of modern America:
1. The unipolar moment was temporary вЂ” strategic competition is the default, not peace.
2. Institutions matter and decay slowly вЂ” Acemoglu and Robinson's framework applies at home, not just abroad.
3. Trade and technology raise aggregate wealth but distribute gains unevenly вЂ” distribution shapes politics.
4. Free societies must keep choosing freedom вЂ” Reagan's warning still applies.
5. Surveillance powers granted in emergency endure вЂ” Hayek's concern about "emergency" expansions of state power was prescient.
6. Sound money matters вЂ” the 2022 inflation confirmed the lesson the 1970s had taught (Friedman).
7. Cultural and economic dislocation breeds populism on both left and right.
8. Big Tech is a new kind of power вЂ” the constitutional framework was not designed for it, and the answer is unsettled.
9. Demographic change reshapes everything вЂ” politics, economy, religion, family.
10. The American experiment is not finished вЂ” every generation chooses whether to renew it.

What endures

The Constitution amended 27 times since 1787 still governs. The Supreme Court still issues binding rulings. The Federal Reserve still sets monetary policy. Federalism still divides power between Washington and 50 states. Free elections still happen on schedule. The First Amendment still constrains the government's ability to regulate speech. These institutions are imperfect, often frustrating, sometimes dangerous вЂ” but they have survived crises that destroyed many other 20th-century democracies.

Tocqueville's challenge (1835): American democracy depended on civic associations, local self-government, religion, and shared moral horizons вЂ” what Robert Putnam (Bowling Alone, 2000) called "social capital." Many of those have weakened. Whether they can be rebuilt is the central political question of the 21st century.

"A republic вЂ” if you can keep it."
вЂ” Benjamin Franklin, 1787 (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46) вЂ” when asked what kind of government the Constitutional Convention had produced.

quiz.q: Which observation best captures the central lesson of American history from the founding to today?
o: America's prosperity is automatic and permanent
Freedom and prosperity depend on durable institutions вЂ” and those institutions must be actively renewed by each generation
quiz.exp: Burke, Tocqueville, Madison, Reagan, Acemoglu and Robinson, and Putnam вЂ” across centuries and political traditions вЂ” converge on this core point: free institutions are the precondition for free people, and they require active maintenance. The American experiment is a long argument for that proposition.
